solution_comments: just like this, sometimes it's automatic, but sometimes you need to manually close the task terminal. |SEP| here’s the translation of the chinese text in your screenshot: 检查服务端 → “checking server side” 终端正在等待输入。 → “the terminal is waiting for input.” 终端将被任务重用，按任意键关闭。 请向终端提供所需的输入。 → “the terminal will be reused by tasks. press any key to close. please provide the required input to the terminal.” in plain english: your program/task is paused because it’s waiting for you to type something into the terminal. |SEP| here’s the translation of the chinese text in your screenshot: 检查服务端 → “checking server side” 终端正在等待输入。 → “the terminal is waiting for input.” 终端将被任务重用，按任意键关闭。 请向终端提供所需的输入。 → “the terminal will be reused by tasks. press any key to close. please provide the required input to the terminal.” in plain english: your program/task is paused because it’s waiting for you to type something into the terminal. i know, but the problem is that sometimes it can exit the task automatically, while other times it blocks the conversation. maybe it's an operational issue with the ai. by the way, has the problem been solved?

solution_comments: today i noticed that the problem seems solved with the current v [inline code] of the extension. now the model name uses the same model code used to pull models (nice), and the context length looks right for each model previously affected by the problem. i'm closing the issue since it looks resolved in the latest stable version. thanks!
clean_text: ollama provider: misreading context length for some models no related issue found for this label and keywork "ollama" (open or not) --- description: i'm highlighting a bug happening for ollama provider, while fetching all the models, and getting detailed infos for all of them. when i add as provider my local ollama (currently having 7 cloud models pulled) i find that the models list reports a wrong/lower context length for some of the models (in particular, in those cases, it seems to be using the default value i've seen hardcoded in the [code involved]( [url] which is [inline code] resulting in the [inline code] text i see). as you can see here. but, if i follow manually the same workflow of the code, and fetch the get [inline code] , and then, for each model, use the "model" (the model code) to fetch post [inline code] passing the [inline code] in the body, i get the correct numbers. ( i'm referring to ollama local endpoints here ) while the code i've seen (the one in the link above) seems prepared to extract the [inline code] props to build the variable name of the context length prop, it doesn't seem to be working for some models. in fact, as you can see, other models in the list, report the correct value. basically, all the 4 models that report 33k in my screenshot, actually have a bigger (way bigger) context length. --- this is a problem, because when i then try to use the model for a big task, the extension trigger the [inline code] feature way to early, disrupting execution and context. --- env info: - copilot chat extension version: 0.39.1 - vs code version: 1.111.0 (commit: [hash] ) - os version: windows 11 (vscode about report [inline code] ) - feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): adding ollama provider - selected model (e.g. gpt 4.1, claude 3.7 sonnet): the problem comes before model usage. - logs: no relevant logs to share steps to reproduce: since the problem happen to me with only some models, i'm not sure about reproduction. let's try with the same cloud model. also, be aware that i'm not sure if you can pull :cloud models without a premium ollama plan. i would expect you can pull but not use... --- 1. install ollama locally 2. execute [inline code] (and eventually another from my screenshot that has a 33k context length for an a/b) 3. go to [inline code] page of the extension 4. add ollama provider with default values (unless your ollama instance is hosted differently) 5. check the context length column in the models table (you should spot some unexpected 33k) 6. verify the actual context length from your local ollama endpoints - get [url] (fetch all models list) [get the "model" field of the model. in this example [inline code] ] - post [url] with body [inline code] 7. realize that while the model specs, and the ollama api says the context length is [inline code] you are seeing 33k in the models list of the extension. 8. (optional) try to engage with one affected model in a long task or conversation, and see how the [inline code] is triggered when near to 33k tokens. this is the bigger problem, i think, from the user point of view. thanks! if you need more infos, i'm here 👍🏼

solution_comments: proposed fix / ux direction: - bubble terminal subagent failures that come back as provider/tool/model rate-limit errors into the parent agent event stream. - attach structured metadata such as [inline code] / [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] , and the subagent name / id. - render the same retry ui at the parent level when the child error is retryable. - add a clickable summary row in the parent thread linking to the failing subagent turn. - preserve the detailed child history, but do not make it the only place the error is visible. - consider a dedicated parent-level message such as: [inline code] that would make the failure discoverable in the main conversation while still preserving the deeper child transcript for debugging.
clean_text: subagent github rate-limit errors do not surface to the parent agent and do not show the "try again" action type: bug does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: n/a (copilot agent/subagent workflow) vs code version: unknown os version: macos steps to reproduce: 1. start a top-level copilot agent task that invokes a subagent. 2. have the subagent hit a github-side rate limit/error. 3. observe the parent/top-level conversation. expected: - the subagent failure should surface in the top-level conversation. - the same retry affordance (for example a "try again" button) should be available from the top-level context. actual: - the top-level conversation does not surface the rate-limit error. - no "try again" affordance appears at the top level. - the only way to see the failure is to open the subagent history and scroll to the bottom. additional notes: - a screenshot is available showing the nested subagent history error state. - this appears to be specifically about error propagation / retry ux from subagent - parent conversation, not just the existence of rate limiting itself.

solution_comments: the issue was corruption in the global storage folder. removing this and having it rebuild solved the issue. i tried to narrow the scope to a specific folder or file but this prooved not to be successful. if i copied the global storage folder over to the re-installed version the problem returned. i have a solution but thought i would share outcomes here.
clean_text: unable to select a model using the model picker (ctrl+alt+period) type: bug 1. open chat. 2. notice that chat shows gemini 3 pro as the model. 3. open the model picker with ctrl+alt+period (.). 4. select gpt-5 mini. 5. frankly, select any other model. you can try using space or enter, neither one works. expected result: the model changes. steps taken to troubleshoot: confirming with reading the title after tabbing back into the input field. i also asked the chat what model it was using and it confirmed no change in model. sometimes the model picker does show a checkbox on the model itself, meaning it would be assumed it is selected but no confirmation is taking place and asking copilot chat confirms the older selection. this feels like a regression of sorts. vs code version: code - clean_text: inline chat gets gpt41 config [inline code] (default) make an inline chat request 🐛 notice that the request is served with gpt41 😱 notice it uses the sd endpoint to make edits details [code block] typescript if (one === other) { return true; } if (!one || !other) { return false; } if (one.length !== other.length) { return false; } for (let i = 0, len = one.length; i < len; i++) { if (!itemequals(one[i], other[i])) { return false; } } return true; } / remove the element at [inline code] by replacing it with the last element. this is faster than [inline code] but changes the order of the array / export function removefastwithoutkeepingorder (array: t[], index: number) { const last = array.length - 1; if (index < last) { array[index] = array[last]; } array.pop(); } / performs a binary search algorithm over a sorted array. array the array being searched. key the value we search for. comparator a function that takes two array elements and returns zero if they are equal, a negative number if the first element precedes the second one in the sorting order, or a positive number if the second element precedes the first one. see { binarysearch2} / export function binarysearch (array: readonlyarray , key: t, comparator: (op1: t, op2: t) = number): number { return binarysearch2(array.length, i = comparator(array[i], key)); } / performs a binary search algorithm over a sorted collection. useful for cases when we need to perform a binary search over something that isn't actually an array, and converting data to an array would defeat the use of binary search in the first place. length the collection length. comparetokey a function that takes an index of an element in the collection and returns zero if the value at this index is equal to the [code block] typescript export function removefastwithoutkeepingorder (array: t[], index: number) { const last = array.length - 1; solution_comments: a small workaround for people facing this issue 1. click on try downloading manually in the error pop up 2. it downloads a extension name.vsixpackage file and the downloaded file is not visible in the install from vsix file selector 3. rename extension name.vsixpackage - extension name.vsix 4. now try using the install from vsix option in the extensions it shows the renamed file 5. select the renamed file and the extension downloads and installs normally

solution_comments: [inline code] fixes the issue, thank you! i think my only remaining nit feedback is that i wouldn't expect the typing to overlap with the backing gray text ( [inline code] ) - i'd expect it to replace or push that backing text. |SEP| glad [inline code] worked out! the ghost text is really good point. it's very difficult though. the reason being ghost text is coming from cli.. but then the ime composition happens in xterm before any of characters are even sent to cli. so until the user commits from ime, cli receives nothing and basically have no idea of typing started and won't really clear the placeholder. if vs code has the ownership of ghost text we could probably fix this, or maybe we could channel this from cli - vs code - xterm.

clean_text: update status bar entry receives focus automatically in screen reader mode problem in screen reader mode, the update status bar entry appears to receive focus automatically. this unexpectedly moves focus and can interrupt the current navigation context. expected behavior the update status bar entry should not automatically receive focus in screen reader mode. actual behavior focus is moved to (or lands on) the update status bar entry without explicit user intent. why this is a problem automatic focus changes are disruptive for assistive technology users and can cause loss of context while reading or interacting with other ui elements. repro steps 1. enable screen reader mode in vs code. 2. start vs code in a state where an update status bar entry is shown. 3. observe initial focus behavior after startup / when the update entry appears. 4. focus is automatically placed on the update status bar entry. notes - this issue is specifically about behavior in screen reader mode. - the update entry should remain available and discoverable, but focus should stay where the user left it unless explicitly moved by the user.

solution_comments: the issue has resolved on my end. i'm not entirely sure what changed — it could be a server-side fix from microsoft (the marketplace api may have stopped returning gzip-encoded responses for my region), or something changed in my network environment. for the record, the root cause was: the marketplace api returned vspackage with content-encoding: gzip, but the response body was not decompressed before being passed to yauzl for zip extraction. this may have been a transient server-side behavior. closing this issue. if anyone encounters the same problem, the workaround is to manually download the vsix with curl --compressed, which forces gzip decompression.

clean_text: allow updating a system/admin vs code install from within vs code type: feature request tl;dr on windows systems where uac is disabled (enablelua = 0), all processes run elevated. in this configuration: the vs code user installer cannot run because it detects elevation. existing system installer installations cannot update automatically. this leaves manual reinstallation of the system installer as the only way to update vs code. it would be helpful if vs code supported updating system installations via an elevation prompt, allowing users in uac-disabled environments to update from within vs code rather than downloading the installer manually. ---------------------------------------------------------------------- environment windows 11 domain-joined machine vs code originally installed using the system installer uac disabled (enablelua = 0) user account is a domain user, not the built-in administrator problem when attempting to update vs code to the latest version, the built-in update mechanism does not work because the installation was performed using the system installer. the obvious alternative is to download and run the user installer, which normally allows updates without administrator privileges. however, when uac is disabled (enablelua = 0), all processes run with elevated privileges. as a result: the user installer refuses to run, displaying: “this user installer is not meant to be run as an administrator.” because uac is disabled, it is not possible to launch a non-elevated process. this means: the user installer cannot run the system installer cannot update automatically the only option is to manually download and reinstall the system installer each time observed behaviour existing vs code install uses the system installer auto-update does not apply because elevation is required attempting to run the user installer fails because the process is detected as elevated disabling elevation is impossible when enablelua = 0 result users in environments where uac is disabled cannot use the vs code user installer and also cannot update a system installer installation from within vs code. updates therefore require manually downloading and reinstalling vs code each time. suggested improvement possible improvements that would resolve this scenario: allow the user installer to run when elevated if the target install is in the user profile. allow vs code to download and apply updates to a system install by prompting for elevation. provide an “update with elevation” option inside vs code when the installation type requires it. why this matters in corporate or domain environments where uac is disabled or restricted by policy, the current behaviour makes updating vs code unnecessarily difficult. allowing an elevation prompt for updates (similar to many other windows applications) would make updates straightforward regardless of installation type. vs code version: code 1.106.3 ( [hash] , 2025-11-25t22:28:18.024z) os version: windows nt x64 10.0.26100 modes:

clean_text: copilot chat: repeated "token expired or invalid" 401 errors requiring restart bug description copilot chat intermittently fails with a 401 "token expired or invalid" error. this has occurred twice within a 3-hour window. the error persists on retry — clicking "try again" returns the same 401. error details [code block] does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled? no — this is specific to the github copilot chat extension. - vs code version: latest stable (march 2026) - - os version: macos 15 (darwin 25.3.0) steps to reproduce 1. use copilot chat normally in vs code 2. 2. after some period of use (variable, within 1-3 hours), the next chat request fails with 401 "token expired or invalid" 3. 3. clicking "try again" does not resolve the error — it returns the same 401 4. 4. the only workaround is to reload vs code or sign out/sign back in to github expected behavior copilot should automatically refresh its authentication token before it expires, or gracefully re-authenticate when a 401 is received, rather than entering an unrecoverable error state. actual behavior the token silently expires and copilot does not attempt to refresh it. every subsequent request (including retries) fails with the same 401 until the user manually intervenes. additional context - this happened twice in 3 hours on the same session - - signing out and back in to github resolves the issue temporarily - - - reloading vs code ( [inline code] ) also works as a workaround - - - - the token refresh mechanism appears to be broken or not triggering reliably

solution_comments: i eventually solved it by downgrading my vs code version. |SEP| uninstall gihub copilot chat. try to reinstall. download the .vsixpackage manually (click on the button if the installation failed), then rename the downloaded extension from .vsixpackage to .vsix. in vs code extensions ... install from vsix... solved the issue for me.
clean_text: github copilot extension v0.39.0 incompatible with vs code 1.111 does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: yes/no - na as this is issue with vs code to copilot only. after updating to the latest version of vs code (1.111), the github copilot extension v0.39.0 becomes incompatible and is disabled by the editor. vs code reports that the extension is not compatible with the current version of the editor even though both components are the latest available versions at the time of writing. this prevents using copilot functionality such as inline suggestions and copilot chat in the editor. - copilot chat extension version: 0.39.0 - vs code version: 1.111.0 - os version: windows nt x64 10.0.26100 steps to reproduce: 1. install or update to vs code 1.111 2. install github copilot extension v0.39.0 3. restart vs code if prompted 4. open the extensions view 2. it doesn't anymore now, with message invalid extension detected expected behavior github copilot extension should load normally and provide inline suggestions and copilot chat functionality. actual behavior vs code shows that the extension is incompatible with the current version and disables it automatically. as a result: copilot suggestions are not available copilot chat cannot be used extension appears disabled due to incompatibility additional context this issue appears to occur immediately after updating vs code to version 1.111 with copilot extension version 0.39.0 installed.

solution_comments: ok so, given the importance of figuring this out quickly due to not getting new strings, i've made a short fix that doesn't make things worse and simplifies things a bit: we now run [this pipeline]( [url] daily, which will check for the latest [inline code] branch, if there's a newer one, it will do what it did before and update the repo to track that new branch. this will bump the engine version so everyone's happy. this means that endgame champ no longer has to run a pipeline to do this. one less step ✅ this means that new strings will only go in when we branch, and with an sla of 3 days, they may be slightly delayed before the release... but that's not too bad. i think it's ok. plus, weekly releases, if a string doesn't make it, oh well, wait a week, it'll be there no problem. not the end of the world. possible improvement of that: release language packs when they change instead of on a schedule. that'll really be reactive... it's probably ok to do this, i think. ✅ [url] we still keep the same "[mirroring of vs code build steps]( [url] which isn't great but it's also what we had before so that's ok tracked: [url]
clean_text: localization process isn't aligned with weekly releases due to the process change of weekly releases, the process for localization of vs code is broken. a lot of it heavily relied on monthly branching: [url] even in our previous process monthly release process, the branching was done by endgame champ. additionally, the pipeline relies on building vs code in order to extract the strings from it... with vs code moving faster, keeping those build steps stable will be difficult and has already given me headaches over the years. with weekly releases this has to be rethought. ideas: can we not do this branch swapping and run the pipeline off of either the current release branch or even main? (this will need to make sure that the engine version is properly reflected in the resulting language packs) can we avoid building vscode in a satellite repo and instead bring this creation of english strings into core and then pass them to a pipeline in vscode-loc-drop? since we are in a broken state today, this is quite important to address as it means no new strings are translated.

clean_text: bug? integrated terminal font rendering appears thin / poorly antialiased in light themes on macos does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: yes/no type: bug platform: macos vs code version: 1.111.0 (observed in recent updates) environment: occurs across multiple macbook devices with different screen resolutions description in recent versions of vs code, the integrated terminal appears to have a font rendering issue when using light themes on macos . when both the editor theme and terminal theme are light , terminal fonts appear overly thin with visible pixel zig-zag artifacts , suggesting that font antialiasing is not being applied correctly. this significantly reduces readability. this behavior does not occur when: - using dark editor themes - using a light editor theme with a dark terminal theme the issue appears to affect all light themes tested , not just a specific theme. observed effects - terminal text appears very thin and jagged - antialiasing artifacts are visible - powerline / nerd fonts icons become difficult to distinguish - tui box-drawing characters become hard to read examples: - the frame around the “openai codex” banner in the screenshot becomes almost illegible. - similar rendering artifacts appear in other terminal-based tools that rely on text ui elements. ![image]( [url] ![image]( [url] steps to reproduce 1. use macos 2. install the latest version of vs code 3. select a light editor theme 4. use a light terminal theme 5. open the integrated terminal 6. use a terminal font such as nerd fonts / powerline fonts 7. run a tui application (example: codex cli) expected behavior terminal fonts should render with proper antialiasing and normal stroke thickness , consistent with how they appear: - in dark vs code themes - in native macos terminal or iterm - in vs code when using dark terminal themes troubleshooting attempted the following were tested but did not resolve the issue : - disabling macos system font antialiasing - enabling or disabling terminal font weight settings - setting the following in [inline code] : [code block] - testing across multiple macbook devices - testing across different display resolutions - testing with multiple light themes [code block]

solution_comments: hi -tree , apologies for the delay, and thanks for reporting this. i tested the code on colab with tensorflow 2.20.0 and was able to [reproduce]( [url] the behavior you are seeing. in eager mode, the model name is not validated as a tensorflow root scope, which is why names containing special characters (like special) appear to work. once the model is traced and compiled with [inline code] , the model name is used internally as a tensorflow root scope. at that point, tensorflow applies its naming rules, and characters like [inline code] are not allowed, so xla raises a [inline code] . this is expected behavior and is not specific to keras or a regression in xla. renaming the model to use only supported characters (for example, [inline code] or [inline code] ) resolves the issue. i have verified this locally and added a colab [gist]( [url] with a working example for reference. thank you!

solution_comments: the issue appears to be caused by mixed dtypes passed to tf.maximum in xla mode. python literals (0, 1) are inferred as int32, while x is float32, which xla does not allow. explicitly matching the dtype resolves the problem without changing behavior: tf.maximum( tf.constant(0.0, dtype=x.dtype), tf.constant(1.0, dtype=x.dtype) - tf.abs(x) ) alternatively, using tf.zeros like / tf.ones like also works. this keeps eager behavior unchanged and makes the code xla-safe.

solution_comments: hi -tree , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising this issue. i ran your code on tensorflow 2.21.0-rc0 and the nightly version. while i encountered the same issues on 2.21.0-rc0, i observed that the problem has been resolved in the nightly build. therefore, this fix will be included in upcoming versions. i have attached a working colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| technical note on root cause and best practices it is great to see that the issue is mitigated in the tf 2.21 nightly build and that the compiler now handles this state without crashing. however, from an architectural standpoint, we should distinguish between a "crash fix" and a "performant implementation." the performance aspect: using python primitives like random.randint to determine shapes often forces the compiler into frequent re-tracing. each time the python value changes, xla may see it as a new graph signature, leading to unnecessary compilation overhead. recommendation for stability and speed: even with the latest fixes, it remains best practice to use native tensorflow semantics (e.g., tf.shape(x)[0]) instead of python-side generators. logical stability: ensures the graph is portable across cpu, gpu, and tpu backends. optimal performance: by using tf.shape, the dimensions remain symbolic within the same compiled executable. this avoids the "re-compilation trap" and allows xla to optimize the hardware kernels once for all input sizes. while the nightly fix prevents the failure, using tf.shape ensures the system remains lean and statically sound.

solution_comments: hi , apologies for the delay, thanks for reporting this issue. the issue is caused by the extremely large tensor size used as input to [inline code] . you are using tensor: [code block] contains more than 2.1 billion elements and requires about 4.3 gb of memory for the input alone. a transpose operation also needs additional temporary buffers, so the total memory requirement easily exceeds 10–15 gb, which is beyond the capacity of typical gpus. additionally, these dimensions exceed the internal indexing limits of the gpu transpose kernel (swapdimension1and2intensor3usingtiles). when the grid size becomes too large, tile indexing arithmetic overflows and the kernel computes invalid memory offsets, which leads to the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when a reasonable tensor size is used (e.g., 512×512, 1024×1024, etc.), the operation works normally. tensorflow should ideally raise a clean resourceexhaustederror or shape validation error, so your report highlights a missing validation check. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a smaller valid example. hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

solution_comments: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for the report. the issue is caused by invalid inputs passed to sparsesegmentsum, rather than a problem with the gpu kernel itself. in your code, i noticed the following: - data has shape [0, 4], which means it contains zero rows. since there are no valid rows, sparsesegmentsum cannot read from this tensor. - indices contains the values [48, 0, 116, 0]. however, with data.shape = [0, 4], all indices are out of range, because no valid index exists. because of these invalid inputs, the gpu kernel attempts to read elements from an empty buffer, which results in the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when valid data, indices, and segment ids are used, the op works correctly. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a corrected example for your reference. i hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

clean_text: [linux] linking tensorflow-lite in app with grpc issue type build/install have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version 2.19 custom code yes os platform and distribution linux ubuntu/fedora/debian mobile device no response python version no response bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? having problems linking with libtensorflow-lite.so. in particular around absl libraries. grpc is being used in the app. grpc is pulled in as a system dependency, which depends on a system absl-cpp dev package version. this causes linking problems with libtensorflow-lite.so. instead of sand boxing absl for linux, it should be using the system absl package; absl-cpp-devel in the case of fedora 42/43. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

solution_comments: the issue is incompatibility with cuda compute 12: one workaround which worked for me is use ngc tf containers which extends support for blackwell architecture: [code block] run and chill |SEP| , thank you for the quick and helpful response to the user. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. currently, tensorflow official releases are tested and built up to cuda 12.5. since cuda 12.8 and blackwell (compute capability 12.0a) are newer, tensorflow wheels currently fall back to jit-compiling ptx kernels, which can result in longer initialization times. as mentioned, using the nvidia ngc tensorflow container is a valid temporary workaround those builds include newer cuda/cudnn versions that extend support for blackwell gpus. you can refer to the official [tensorflow compatibility documentation]( [url] here for the latest supported cuda and cudnn versions. thank you!

solution_comments: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here. i checked your code, and the main cause appears to be the extremely large tensor shape you are using. as observed, the tensor size allocates over 8 gb of gpu memory, which can easily exhaust or fragment available gpu resources. when gpu memory is exhausted or fragmented, compute-sanitizer may incorrectly report this as an “invalid read.” i verified this on my side with smaller tensors using tensorflow versions 2.20.0 and 2.19.0, and it worked fine without any errors. i have also attached a colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. please try using smaller tensor shapes to confirm that the issue is related to memory size rather than a kernel bug. hope this helps! thank you.

solution_comments: """ fix: invalid k parameter in nearestneighbors operation issue: k=-1 caused invalid argument error with message "expected shape dimensions to be non-negative, got - [hash] " root cause: - k parameter represents number of nearest neighbors to find - negative values are mathematically invalid for this operation - tensorflow's dimension validation rejects negative shape dimensions solution: - changed k from -1 to 1 (find single nearest neighbor) - k must be positive integer where 0 < k < = number of centers """ k = tf.constant(1, dtype=tf.int64) |SEP| hi thank you so much for taking the time to analyze and fix this issue — really appreciate it! this was actually my first tensorflow bug report, and i’m really glad it helped improve the library. it would mean a lot if i could be mentioned or tagged as the reporter/contributor for this issue. thanks again for the detailed response and learning opportunity!

solution_comments: hi, this issue occurs because keras.layers.lstm with default parameters uses the cudnn fast path, which is not supported by xla gpu jit. a simple workaround is to disable the cudnn path by setting recurrent dropout 0. for example: layers.lstm(32, recurrent dropout=0.1, return sequences=false) this allows the lstm to compile and run under xla on gpu while still functioning correctly. this works in my ubuntu 24.04 [issue 100872.py]( [url] |SEP| hi -jain7, thanks for your pointers and quick response. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. i was able to reproduce the same behavior with tensorflow 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. as -jain7 mentioned, setting recurrent dropout 0 disables the cudnn fast path and allows the lstm to compile under xla gpu jit. i tried this workaround and it works fine for me as well. here is a minimal [gist]( [url] with the workaround for reference. thank you!

solution_comments: -google i'm not sure how [url] could break mac build since all options shouldn't affect mac build in any way to my understanding (they don't touch select case for mac build at all). introduces version script which still exports everything it doesn't. it shouldn't change what is exported, just add [inline code] version to all already global symbols. in other words, the version script part did not change the behavior, the symbolic-functions arg broke mac build, and i don't think the symbolic optimization technique is appropriate for a binary which is being loaded all over the place (both automatically via dynamic section resolution and often manually by dlopen() in downstream projects), as it is likely to brake downstream projects in weird ways. this pr was aimed at fixing tf already breaking in weird ways due to symbol interposition (which the symbolic flag is supposed to fix). if tensorflow is used by the application that already links with llvm libraries (common case), it can break in non-trivial ways because tf so files would end up using linked llvm instead of the llvm they were statically linked to (and they were linked against unversioned llvm symbols due to static llvm linking so the consumer using versioned llvm on their end wouldn't help). another way to fix it is to carefully control what is exported and mark all thirdparty symbols as hidden but it looks like the build already deteriorated in that regard so it'd be non-trivial from someone with no in-depth knowledge of the project to do. i.e. [inline code] depends on llvm ostream symbols from [inline code] (probably unintentionally, but that's why i had to change the linker script since the old one stopped working at some point because it was forgotten/not included in the pywrap refactor). so to solve it either the code would need to be modified or tensorflow cc should end up being linked to some static libs that framework already links to. |SEP| [-google]( [url] i'm not sure how []( [url] could break mac build since all options shouldn't affect mac build in any way to my understanding (they don't touch select case for mac build at all). i think this is because of solution_comments: hi and , i totally understand that it would be convenient for use cases like molecular dynamics, sensitivity analysis, and ode solvers if these function transforms could work under torch.inference mode(). however, i believe we should raise an explicit error instead of silently returning incorrect zeros or trying to make them work. torch.inference mode() is explicitly designed for inference-only workloads with autograd fully disabled. running gradient-related transforms like grad, vjp, and jvp inside this mode is semantically inconsistent. allowing them to run silently not only produces wrong results but also violates the design contract of inference mode. for users who need differentiation while avoiding unnecessary gradient tracking, torch.no grad() is already the appropriate choice. therefore, i think the correct fix is to detect when these apis are used under inference mode() and raise a clear error message to prevent silent failures. |SEP| it might be useful to add this to the docs on tue, mar 17, 2026, 9:04 am lillian @ . wrote: lillian68 left a comment (pytorch/pytorch) hi and , i totally understand that it would be convenient for use cases like clean_text: torch.func transforms silently return zeros under inference mode 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] silently produce incorrect results (zeros) when called under [inline code] . no error is raised. [code block] root cause functorch transforms set up their own autograd context on entry: enable grad() for grad/vjp, set fwd grad enabled(true) for jvp (see note [grad and vjp interaction with no grad]). however, inference mode also excludes autograd dispatch keys from the tls dispatch key set, and the mode-enabling calls don't undo that. so autograd dispatch keys remain excluded and differentiation silently produces zeros. the transforms already correctly handle no grad — this is the same class of issue with a different tls mechanism. expected behavior either produce correct results (by also disabling inference mode on transform entry, matching the existing no grad handling) or raise an error. cc

solution_comments: fixing this particular problem is easy, but i would like a follow up where we look for other parenthesization problems, and some thought about how to prevent this in the future. i think the reason i didn't add a pair of parenthesis is, i dnd't realized that num split/rnumel can be additive sympy expressions. maybe we should always be conservative in future when we construct sympy expression strings. i don't really have other good ideas to systematically avoid this kind of issues. we could build something like 'expressionbuilder' to construct sympy expression as [inline code] and take care of the precedence. but 1. people can skip using this builder class and go ahead and construct the expression manually 2. this is not as easy to code as the manual way pr [url] fix this specific issue but does not handle the general problem |SEP| i'm not sure how to fix issues like the one above when raw sympy expressions used in codegen strings bypass pexpr, but for ones that go through pexpr, we can just make pythonprinter.doprint wrap compound expressions in parenthesis before returning. we can check what needs to be wrapped by checking precedence values. look at the below code snippet for an example of why this would work. but this would correctly handle add, mul, floordiv, pythonmod, inttruediv while leaving other function-call expressions unwrapped. this would lead to a case where extra parenthesis are printed but it is still valid python. i actually think this might have been a planned change if you look at the pythonprinter.doprint, we have an unused boolean variable p. we would need to fix a lot of the test cases if this fix is implemented as well, but i think it would work. [code block] output: s0 precedence=1000 is atom=true

solution_comments: this is likely to be expected behavior since kaiming uniform and uniform generate random numbers by iterating over the underlying storage. if the memory layout differs, the same random values get mapped to different logical positions in the tensor. a workaround would be to - create the model in regular format - initialize it - then convert the model to be channels last |SEP| i have implemented the native fix for this inconsistency by ensuring initialization happens on a contiguous buffer, as discussed. i've also added a regression test (linked above) that verifies bit-wise parity across memory formats. the pr is now ready for a final review.

clean_text: dtensor shard- replicate redistribution corrupts symbolic shapes under torch.compile 🐛 describe the bug when a dtensor with shard(dim) placement on a dynamically-sized dimension is redistributed to replicate, the gathered dimension's symbolic shape becomes n ceil(s/n) instead of s. this corrupted expression propagates through all downstream operations (view, transpose, reshape, to local), causing shape mismatches in compilation pipelines. root cause: shard. to replicate tensor in placement types.py 1. torch.chunk uses ceiling division: local size = (s + n - 1) // n 2. all gather concatenates n chunks: gathered = n ((s + n - 1) // n) 3. maybe unpad tensor checks "s % n != 0" — evaluates false for even s, so no unpadding occurs 4. result has symbolic size n ceil(s/n) ≠ s clean_text: [release 2.11][vllm] distributed failures - free memory on device cuda:0 (19.77/22.05 gib) on startup is less than desired gpu memory utilization (0.9, 19.84 gib) 24.02.2026: [url] analysis: title: pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0 increases baseline gpu memory usage, breaking memory-tight workloads environment: - pytorch 2.11.0+cu130 - cuda 13.0 / driver 570.133.20 - nvidia l4 (22.05 gib) - ray distributed executor - vllm (pipeline parallel test) description: after upgrading to pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0, the baseline gpu memory consumed before any model loading has increased compared to previous pytorch/cuda versions. on an nvidia l4 (22.05 gib total), only 19.77 gib is free after cuda context initialization, whereas previously enough memory was available for a 90% utilization target (19.84 gib). the 70 mib increase in baseline memory usage causes workloads that target gpu memory utilization=0.9 to fail with an oom-style error during device initialization, before any model weights are loaded. this occurs in a ray distributed setup (2 gpus, pp=2) running microsoft/phi-3.5-moe-instruct with dummy weights. the same configuration worked on prior pytorch + cuda versions. expected behavior: baseline cuda context memory consumption should not increase significantly between pytorch releases, or at minimum should be documented as a known change. reproduction: initialize cuda on an l4 gpu with pytorch 2.11+cu130 via ray workers and check torch.cuda.mem get info() — free memory is lower than with pytorch 2.10 or earlier. [code block] cc

clean_text: masked scatter flattens the source array on mps (apple silicon) 🐛 describe the bug code behavior is different between mps and cpu/cuda. the bug is only relevant to mps device (apple silicon). tensor.masked scatter (mask, source) copies elements from source into self tensor at positions where the mask is true. elements from source are copied into self starting at position 0 of source and continuing in order one-by-one for each occurrence of mask being true. the shape of mask must be [broadcastable]( [url] with the shape of the underlying tensor. the source should have at least as many elements as the number of ones in mask. a minimal example to reproduce a bug: [code block] expected behavior: the behavior of 'masked scatter' is consistent across different accelerator devices. versions pytorch version: 2.7.0 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: none rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: macos 26.3 (arm64) gcc version: could not collect clang version: 17.0.0 (clang-1700.6.3.2) cmake version: version 4.2.3 libc version: n/a python version: 3.10.19 (main, feb 4 2026, 00:02:50) [clang 21.1.4 ] (64-bit runtime) python platform: macos-26.3-arm64-arm-64bit is cuda available: false cuda runtime version: no cuda cuda module loading set to: n/a gpu models and configuration: no cuda nvidia driver version: no cuda cudnn version: no cuda is xpu available: false hip runtime version: n/a miopen runtime version: n/a is xnnpack available: true caching allocator config: n/a cpu: apple m4 pro versions of relevant libraries: [pip3] numpy==2.2.6 [pip3] torch==2.7.0 [pip3] torchaudio==2.7.0 [pip3] torchmetrics==1.8.2 [pip3] torchvision==0.22.0 [conda] could not collect cc

clean_text: [release 2.11][vllm] quantization - test pre quantized model, torchao ci: [url] - 19.02.2026 test pre quantized model summary: the error: assert config.version == 2, f"unexpected version: {config.version}" assertionerror: unexpected version: 1 the model drisspg/fp8-opt-125m was quantized with an older torchao that used float8 config version 1, but the torchao version in your torch 2.11 image expects version 2. this is a breaking change in torchao's float8 dynamic activation float8 weight quantize tensor. the flow is: 1. torchaoconfig.from config detects is checkpoint torchao serialized = true (the model has quant method: torchao) 2. during create weights, it calls torchao quantize param data → quantize () to pre-quantize weights 3. torchao's quantize () hits the version assertion because the model's config is v1 this isn't something fixable in vllm — the model checkpoint itself is incompatible with the newer torchao. the options are: 1. re-quantize the model with the new torchao and push an updated drisspg/fp8-opt-125m 2. skip the test for this torch version (if this is a ci concern) 3. use a different fp8 test model that was quantized with the newer torchao [code block] cc

solution_comments: i think the issue is that there would be a high likelihood that if someone ever made a change that affected the 'protocol' (which is a nebulous concept since there are multiple comms layers with their own protocols), they would probably not know to update the 'version' tag. i don't think it would be all that useful to just have one version tag per pytorch release either. also, we'd need to include things like nccl version or gloo version, which are underlying libraries used by pytorch. so i think this is probably not too actionable.
clean_text: proposal: explicit protocol version compatibility check in torch.distributed.init process group hi all, while reviewing the distributed initialization path, i was thinking about cross-version cluster scenarios (for example, mixed pytorch versions across ranks during rolling upgrades or partially upgraded environments). specifically: rank 0 running pytorch version x another rank running pytorch version y potential subtle differences in collective, rpc, or initialization semantics it seems possible that mismatched protocol expectations could result in: silent hangs unexpected collective errors hard-to-debug runtime mismatches i may be misunderstanding existing guarantees here, so i wanted to ask: 1. is there already an explicit protocol compatibility mechanism between distributed ranks during init process group()? 2. if not, would it be reasonable to introduce a minimal protocol version constant exchanged during initialization in order to fail fast on incompatible revisions? the goal would not necessarily be strict version equality, but rather to: provide a clear error when incompatible protocol revisions are detected avoid subtle cross-version failure modes before experimenting with an implementation, i wanted to get feedback from maintainers on: whether this scenario is already handled elsewhere whether such a guard would align with distributed design goals thanks! cc -huang

clean_text: avoid cuda sync in spectral decompositions 🚀 the feature, motivation and pitch current docs of spectral decompositions say that they require a device < -- cpu sync. i don't know if it's because of the sync, but in practice i am able to compute spectral decompositions for a batch of matrices using cupy interop via dlpack approximatley 10 times faster than with pytorch - and this includes a custom autograd function that also implements backprop for me (500x100x100 tensor, of 500 matrices of size 100x100). i am assuming that if cupy does it quickly, then nvidia already provided some primitives to do it quickly. so i am asking, if it's possible, to use them in pytorch internally as well. alternatives continue using cupy/dlpack interop. additional context i am working on researching trainable models whose nonlinear activations are matrix eigenvalues. making it fast will significantly accelerate my experiments without any special cupy hacks. cc

clean_text: armv8.1 lse regression for armv8.0 devices 🐛 describe the bug pytorch 2.10.0 crashes on armv8.0 processors (cortex-a53/a72/a73) due to lse (large system extensions) instructions in libc10.so. this is a regression - issue reported this problem and claimed it was fixed in 2.4.1, but the issue has returned in 2.10.0. environment • hardware: arm cortex-a53 (armv8.0 architecture) • cpu features: fp asimd evtstrm aes pmull sha1 sha2 crc32 cpuid (notably missing lse) • os: debian trixie (testing), linux 6.12.63 • architecture: aarch64 (arm64) • pytorch version: 2.10.0 • installation method: pip (pip install torch) • python version: 3.13 error description any attempt to import or use pytorch results in sigill (illegal instruction) due to lse atomic instructions that are not supported on armv8.0 processors. error message illegal instruction (core dumped) gdb backtrace program received signal sigill, illegal instruction. 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so (gdb) bt 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe2755d0 in mi process init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe27560c in mi process load () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbf7df180 in call init (env= , argv= , argc= , l= ) at ./elf/dl-init.c:74 call init (l= , argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:26 0x0000ffffbf7df2b8 in dl init (main map=0xffffbf7ff040, argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:121 0x0000ffffbf7fa660 in dl start user () from /lib/ld-linux-aarch64.so.1 disassembly at crash point (gdb) x/5i $pc = 0xffffbe27f3c8 < mi options init+1064 : ldaddal x1, x2, [x0] ; ← lse instruction (sigill) 0xffffbe27f3cc < mi options init+1068 : cmp x2, x0 0xffffbe27f3d0 < mi options init+1072 : b.ne 0xffffbe27f014 < mi options init+100 0xffffbe27f3d4 < mi options init+1076 : adrp x0, 0xffffbe465000 0xffffbe27f3d8 < mi options init+1080 : add x0, x0, x9c8 the ldaddal instruction is part of the armv8.1 lse extension and is not supported on armv8.0 processors like cortex-a53. analysis lse instruction count in libc10.so $ objdump -d /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so | \ grep -e 'ldadd|ldclr|ldeor|ldset|swp|cas' | wc -l 22 the library contains 22 lse instructions, making it completely incompatible with armv8.0 processors. root cause: mimalloc dependency the crash occurs in mi options init() which is part of mimalloc (microsoft's memory allocator). pytorch's c10 library links against mimalloc-static: from c10/cmakelists.txt: if(use mimalloc) target link libraries(c10 private mimalloc-static) endif() it appears that the mimalloc build included in the pytorch wheel is compiled with lse instructions enabled, likely via -march=armv8.1-a or -march=armv8-a+lse flags. steps to reproduce 1. use any armv8.0 processor (cortex-a53, a57, a72, a73 - common in raspberry pi 3/4, many sbcs, older servers) 2. verify cpu lacks lse: grep -o 'atomics\|lse' /proc/cpuinfo (should return nothing) 3. install pytorch 2.10.0: pip install torch 4. try to import: python3 -c "import torch" 5. observe sigill crash regression information this is a regression from the fix mentioned in issue : • original issue: reported lse incompatibility on armv8.0 • claimed fix: said to be resolved in pytorch 2.4.1 • regression: the problem has returned in pytorch 2.10.0 the lse instructions should not be present in builds targeting general arm64 compatibility. the standard -march=armv8-a (without +lse) should be used for maximum compatibility. versions collecting environment information... pytorch version: n/a is debug build: n/a cuda used to build pytorch: n/a rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: debian gnu/linux 13 (trixie) (aarch64) gcc version: (debian 14.2.0-19) 14.2.0 clang version: could not collect cmake version: version 3.31.6 libc version: glibc-2.41 python version: 3.13.5 (main, jun 25 solution_comments: i looked into this and i think here's what's happening: the expression [inline code] gets lowered to [inline code] + [inline code] + [inline code] . the issue is in how inductor handles [inline code] with duplicate indices. [url] and within [inline code] : [url] here [inline code] creates a [inline code] ir node that iterates over the index tensor [inline code] . with [inline code] (non-atomic stores), the generated kernel processes indices sequentially. since [inline code] reads from [inline code] , iteration 2 sees the already-modified value from iteration 1: [code block] eager mode evaluates the rhs fully before any writes, giving [inline code] . that said, per the [docs]( [url] "if accumulate is false, the behavior is undefined if indices contain duplicate elements." so this might technically be within spec - though users generally expect [inline code] to match eager. to fix this, i think inductor would need to detect when [inline code] reads from [inline code] (perhaps via [inline code] ) and fall back to the non-optimized aten path. this would sacrifice fusion/vectorization for correctness in this edge case.

clean_text: [heads-up] pytorch 2.9.x conv3d regression may significantly slow qwen3-vl vision forward this is not a qwen3-vl implementation bug, but an upstream pytorch behavior that may affect qwen3-vl users. we observed that upgrading pytorch from 2.8.x to 2.9.x can cause a 10–20× slowdown in vision-conditioned forward / generation for qwen3-vl, while text-only / llm-only inference remains unaffected. root cause (analysis) qwen3-vl vision patch embedding uses a conv3d layer (model.visual.patch embed.proj). on pytorch 2.9.1 (cuda 12.8), this conv3d may fall back to aten::slow conv dilated3d for certain shapes / gpus, instead of using a fast cudnn kernel. profiling shows this single conv3d dominates the entire vision forward. this behavior has been reported upstream in pytorch and appears to be resolved in pytorch 2.10. practical suggestions - if you see severe vision-side slowdowns, consider staying on pytorch 2.8.x - or upgrade to pytorch 2.10+ - be cautious when benchmarking qwen3-vl vision performance on pytorch 2.9.x posting this as a heads-up so users are aware of the upstream interaction. cc

solution_comments: i'm not 100% sure. at the risk of being too verbose, the way i wrote [inline code] is: one thread is created for each element of the input matrix $a$ (really it can be a batch of matrices, but i'll focus on the single matrix case). then for each iteration in [inline code] , each thread does the following steps: 1. calculate one element of $h i$, which is a matrix of the same size as $a$ which depends only on the values of $a$ and $i$. each thread writes the element to a buffer. this buffer is reused on each iteration. 2. [inline code] 3. calculate one element of a matmul $h {0...i} = h {0...i-1} h i$, where $h {0...i-1}$ is the result from the previous iteration. 4. [inline code] 5. write the calculated element of $h {0...i}$ to the result buffer. the barrier at step 2 is necessary to make sure all elements of $h i$ are written to memory before the matmul is calculated by any of the threads. barrier at step 4 is to make sure all threads wait until the whole matmul is calculated before any threads go ahead to calculate the next $h i$ and overwrite the values in the existing buffer for it before all threads got a chance to read the previous value out. if $a$ (and thus $h i$) has more elements in it than the number of threads in a threadgroup, then to me it seems like the barrier won't properly sync all the threads the way the algorithm requires. or is there something else i'm missing now? if there really is a race, then i think the solution is probably to make only one thread group operate on $a$, and each thread can calculate multiple elements if the number of elements is greater than the maximum thread group size. but of course if $a$ is batched, we can create a separate thread group for each batch. let me know what you think |SEP| i think your concern is valid, and the ambiguity here is exactly where correctness bugs tend to hide. the key question seems to be the synchronization scope, not metal vs cuda per se. [inline code] only provides ordering within a single threadgroup. if [inline code] (and thus [inline code] ) spans more elements than can be covered by one threadgroup, then multiple threadgroups will necessarily participate in producing or consuming [inline code] . in that case, the barriers at steps (2) and (4) cannot guarantee the global ordering that the algorithm assumes. in other words, the algorithm requires: 1. all elements of [inline code] to be fully materialized before any thread computes [inline code] 2. all elements of [inline code] to be fully consumed before any thread overwrites the buffer for [inline code] these are global synchronization requirements. a threadgroup-level barrier is insufficient unless we can prove that a single threadgroup owns the entire matrix (or batch slice). i agree this is not unique to metal — cuda has the same limitation — but cuda implementations typically avoid this pattern by either: 1. enforcing one block per matrix (with each thread handling multiple elements), or 2. introducing kernel boundaries between iterations to obtain a global sync. so if the current mps implementation allows multiple threadgroups to participate in a single [inline code] , then this is very likely a real race, not just a theoretical one. the fact that it may “usually work” would be consistent with a latent synchronization bug. your proposed fix (one threadgroup per matrix, with striding inside the group) seems like the safest correctness-preserving approach, especially for non-batched or small-batch cases. kernel-splitting would also be a correctness-first alternative if performance allows. happy to be corrected if there's a guarantee that [inline code] never crosses a threadgroup boundary — but if not, i think this deserves a fix or at least a documented constraint. |SEP| i wrote the following script to try to find out for sure if there is a race condition: [code block] this calls [inline code] with increasing matrix sizes. for each size, it calls the op 100 times and prints out the largest element-wise error between the mps and cpu results. to try to minimize effects of normal floating point error, the input matrix is the identity matrix with one random element set to 1. this is the output i get when i run it: [code block] as soon as the matrix reaches a size with more than 1024 elements, i start seeing significant errors. the system i ran this on has [inline code] . i'll write a fix and i'll add a test similar to the above

solution_comments: i have conducted additional testing on pytorch 2.10.0+cu126 to investigate this issue. the experimental results are as follows: test configuration: pytorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 layer: lstm (input size=50, hidden size=50, num layers=1, batch first=true) test modes: mode a: cuda eager (cuda/f32/0000) - baseline mode b: cpu compile (cpu/f32/1000) with inductor fallback random mode c: cuda compile (cuda/f32/1000) with inductor fallback random mode d: cpu eager (cpu/f32/0000) experimental results: [code block] key findings: the issue is not related to compilation: mode c (cuda compile) shows perfect alignment (0% error) with mode a (cuda eager), indicating that torch.compile works correctly on cuda. the divergence is between cpu and cuda implementations: both cpu eager (mode d) and cpu compile (mode b) exhibit a 200% relative error compared to cuda eager, and they produce identical results (1.52e+00), suggesting the issue lies in the cpu implementation itself. severity of the issue: even if the input data represents edge cases or boundary values, a 200% relative error between cpu and cuda implementations is unacceptable. this level of divergence can lead to: error accumulation in deep models with multiple lstm layers final prediction label changes when the same model runs on cpu vs cuda inconsistent behavior across different hardware platforms this is a significant issue that cannot be ignored, as it directly impacts model reproducibility and cross-platform consistency. i can provide additional test data, input files, or any other materials needed for further investigation. thank you for your attention to this matter. |SEP| thank you for this great contribution! this finding is crucial—it proves the issue goes beyond just the compiler. if you need any test data or additional reproduction cases, feel free to ask. i hope this issue gets resolved soon.

solution_comments: hello there. looking at it i can confirm this looks like an api contract issue between [inline code] and backend-specific stream/event apis. in particular: - [inline code] returns a generic [inline code] , not a backend-native stream. - [inline code] (cuda / xpu) appears to assume some backend-native stream and attempts a direct downcast. - on cuda this fails with a misleading [inline code] error. - on xpu the lack of validation leads to a segmentation fault, which should never happen at the python api level. it’s reasonable to expect that a stream returned by [inline code] is either: - accepted by backend [inline code] , or - rejected with a clear python exception explaining the incompatibility. at minimum, this seems to warrant: - explicit runtime type checks in [inline code] (especially xpu), and/or - documentation clarifying that [inline code] is not interchangeable with backend-native streams. happy to help with a fix or documentation update if maintainers think that would be useful. |SEP| thanks for your investigation. -intel i drafted a pr [url] to fix it.

solution_comments: if we have to have a solution from fsdp2 side, the solution is [inline code] . it will keep transformer parameters unsharded during backward. if we own cache implementation, cache should be able to do this: torch.tensor - fsdpmodule - fsdpmodule.unshard() before backward - fsdpmodule.reshard() after backward see my script to use [inline code] to workaround [code block] |SEP| closing my pr, reshard after forward=false fixes the issue on the original repro code |SEP| hey folks — i’d ask you to take another look at this, as it seems to be broader than just independent / unused outputs. consider a model distillation setup (e.g., [dmd]( [url] for diffusion models) where we have a very large teacher model wrapped with fsdp using [inline code] . the model cannot be materialized on a single gpu. during the “fake” pass (when computing both scores), the teacher is run under [inline code] . with [inline code] , the model remains sharded after each layer forward; otherwise, it gets gathered and immediately ooms. the issue is that running under [inline code] prevents the teacher from participating in the loss computation, which then results in an error. however, reshard after forward=true is required here to avoid the gather/oom. rewrapping or restructuring the teacher model is not a practical option, as we don’t own its implementation — it can be a model from e.g. [inline code] . please note that this behavior was not an issue in [inline code] . to reproduce the pattern in a minimal setup, you can replace [inline code] with: [code block]

solution_comments: core issue: batch size dependency the same sample produces correct output when processed individually ( [inline code] ), but produces nan when processed as part of a batch. this violates sample independence - a sample's output should not depend on other samples in the batch. key observation: - single sample: [inline code] → correct output (max: 9.95e-01) - same sample in batch: [inline code] → nan this batch size-dependent behavior causes non-reproducible results and inconsistency between different batch configurations. the fix should ensure sample independence regardless of batch size.

solution_comments: pytorch github issue - custom autograd function support in dynamo exporter ✅ submitted: [url] date: january 25, 2026 ✅ workaround implemented: january 26, 2026 using the [inline code] approach recommended by the onnx team. --- issue summary the new [inline code] exporter fails when models use custom [inline code] classes that access [inline code] in the backward pass. error [code block] --- ✅ workaround: torch.library.custom op reference: [url] the onnx team recommended using [inline code] instead of [inline code] . this approach is fully compatible with dynamo onnx export. implementation for darkir we created onnx-compatible versions of darkir's problematic components: 1. replace [inline code] with standard pytorch ops original (not onnx compatible): [code block] onnx-compatible option 1: pure pytorch [code block] onnx-compatible option 2: groupnorm wrapper (fastest) [code block] 2. replace fft operations with cnn approximations original (not onnx compatible pre-2.6): [code block] onnx-compatible: multi-scale cnn [code block] files created | file | purpose | | [inline code] | onnx-compatible layernorm2d implementations | | [inline code] | onnx-compatible eblock/dblock building blocks | | [inline code] | complete onnx-compatible darkir model | | [inline code] | export script with weight transfer | validation results [code block] --- lessons learned 1. [inline code] is not supported by dynamo onnx exporter - use pure pytorch ops instead - or use [inline code] for complex cases 2. [inline code] is equivalent to layernorm - this is the fastest onnx export path - groupnorm is a standard onnx operator 3. fft operations supported in pytorch 2.6+ / opset 17+ - for older targets, use cnn approximations 4. weight transfer requires careful key mapping - [inline code] → [inline code] for groupnorm wrapper - fremlp weights don't transfer (different architecture) --- related links - pytorch issue: [url] - custom ops tutorial: [url] - onnx dynamo docs: [url] --- resolution: implemented workaround using pure pytorch ops and groupnorm wrapper. the onnx team's recommendation to use [inline code] is the official approach for complex cases.

pytorch version: 2.5.1 (also tested with 2.9.1) - python version: 3.12.10 - onnx version: 1.20.1 - onnxscript version: 0.5.7 - os: windows 11 - cuda: 11.8 / 12.1 --- 🔍 context why this matters many video restoration and computer vision models use custom autograd functions for: - performance optimization (faster backward pass) - numerical stability (custom gradient computation) - memory efficiency (selective tensor saving) examples of affected models: - darkir (cvpr 2025) - video restoration - restoreformer - face restoration - basicvsr++ - video super-resolution - many models with custom layernorm, instancenorm, or attention mechanisms related issues - ✅ resolved: []( [url] - fft operations now supported in dynamo exporter (pytorch 2.6+) - ❓ this issue: custom autograd functions not supported the fft issue was resolved, but custom autograd remains a blocker for onnx export. --- 💡 possible solutions option 1: add support for [inline code] allow dynamo to handle [inline code] / [inline code] unpacking in backward passes. option 2: provide workaround documentation document how to rewrite custom autograd functions for dynamo compatibility: - use standard pytorch ops only - avoid manual [inline code] / [inline code] - show examples of equivalent implementations option 3: fallback to torchscript for custom ops allow dynamo exporter to fall back to torchscript export for submodules with custom autograd, similar to how it handles unsupported ops. --- 🎯 requested action 1. investigate if custom autograd function support can be added to dynamo exporter 2. document workarounds if architectural limitations prevent support 3. provide guidance on rewriting custom functions for onnx compatibility --- 📎 additional information full stack trace click to expand full traceback [code block] workaround attempts ❌ tried: using [inline code] instead of [inline code] - same error ❌ tried: [inline code] the custom function - still fails in dynamo ✅ working: pytorch jit compilation (torchscript) - but no npu/directml support 🤝 contribution happy to provide more test cases or help with documentation if needed! use case: video restoration application for vhs/analog tape digitization - need npu/directml support for efficient batch processing. --- cc

solution_comments: same error. fix please |SEP| i can reproduce this issue on pytorch 2.10.0. the [inline code] handle is not properly traced inside a [inline code] ’d function: eager execution returns a valid handle, while the compiled version either fails or produces an incorrect constant value. hypothesis on root cause: 1. non-tensor object: the stream handle ( [inline code] ) is a python integer / [inline code] , not a [inline code] . dynamo is primarily designed to trace tensor-based computations, so this value may be treated as unsupported or incorrectly constant-folded. 2. runtime state dependency: the handle depends on the current cuda runtime state. during tracing, dynamo may assume it is invariant and fold it as a constant, or trigger a graph break with undefined behavior. 3. regression: this behavior worked in pytorch 2.9.1, so this appears to be a regression in how [inline code] or the [inline code] property is intercepted by the compilation pipeline. suggested workaround (if acceptable for the use case): - query the cuda stream outside the compiled region, and keep [inline code] limited to tensor operations. - alternatively, explicitly isolate the stream query behind a graph break so it executes eagerly. example workarounds: python workaround 1: query outside the compiled function current stream handle = torch.cuda.current stream().cuda stream .compile def f(x): x = x + 1 return x workaround 2: isolate the stream query via a graph break class streamquerymodule(torch.nn.module): def forward(self, x): stream handle = torch.cuda.current stream().cuda stream eager x = x + 1 return stream handle, x i’m happy to provide additional debugging output (e.g., torch logs="+dynamo") if that would help pinpoint where the incorrect constant or failure is introduced. |SEP| hi all, i will take a look at this. i'm curious what you were using streams in your code when we don't respect these in torch.compile? i've been working on a larger project to get torch.compile to respect stream assignments as well so this will get fixed. |SEP| thanks for the context, i will make sure my fix handles this case as well. |SEP| this is fixed by [url]

clean_text: [dtensor]inconsistent random number generation in torch.distributed.tensor.randn 🐛 describe the bug bug description: when using torch.distributed.tensor.randn to create dtensors with fixed random seeds, the generated tensors are inconsistent between single-gpu and multi-gpu scenarios. specifically, creating a (4, 4) tensor on a single gpu produces different results compared to creating (2, 4) tensors on two gpus separately, even when using the same random seed. expected behavior: with the same random seed, the distributed tensor creation should maintain consistency with single-device semantics. the concatenation of multi-gpu results should match the single-gpu result when using equivalent global shapes and seeds. actual behavior: the random values generated in distributed mode differ from single-device mode, breaking reproducibility and debugging capabilities. versions torch 2.9.1 cuda 12.4 python scripy [inline code] cc -huang -l

solution_comments: i was hoping the hsa-rocr 7.2.0 update would fix this issue, but it's still doing the exact same thing. i created a new conda environment from scratch and just install pytorch and the exact same thing is happening. i have no clue what's going on and have just about given up on fixing it. everything on the system is working but pytorch and i can use rocm to render scenes in blender without any issue. |SEP| i found [this issue]( [url] that appears to be giving the exact same seq fault as what i'm seeing. it appears that they were having an issue with kernel versions after 6.18.3, which was around the same time that i updated my pc. it looked like people were hoping that it would be fixed with rocm 7.2, but i'm currently running 7.2.0-1 and it still's not working. if this is the issue, the only fix is to roll it back to 6.18.3 or wait until they eventually fix it. i'm waiting for 6.19.1 to be released. if that doesn't fix it, i'll try to roll it back and see if that helps. i'm still confused as to why this only seems to affect torch and not blender. |SEP| there was an update to linux, not to 6.19.1, but still an update. so i installed the update and there was no change; i was still getting seq faults. i then rolled back the linux package to 6.18.3, which is the version that i heard worked for another person, and everything started working perfectly again! everything on the system has the latest version except the kernel, so the issue is one of two things. the linux kernel broke something after 6.18.3 for gfx1151. or, torch needs to be updated for the change in the kernel after 6.18.3. i'm highly leaning on this being just a kernel issue but i'll leave it up to the torch devs to determine if this is the case and whether this should be closed or stay open. i don't know the internal workings of torch enough to know if this is something that could have been caused by torch, so i don't feel comfortable closing the issue if there's still a possibility of torch causing this. |SEP| thanks again for all the details and for keeping us posted. have you tried therock-based pytorch wheels to see if that solves your issue (similar to therock issue) that you referenced? here are some instructions for install therock-based pytorch wheels: [url] it looks like latest pytorch with nightly therock can be installed as follows: [code block] it looks like some stable versions are available as well. |SEP| sorry about the late response. this morning i updated to 7.2 pytorch for the nightly build and then updated my pc with the latest linux kernel 6.19.6. everything worked fines now, so i'm going to close this issue. thanks for all the help to everyone that helped on this.

clean_text: triton wheels don't have hashes in urls 🐛 describe the bug currently the triton wheels url format in the pytorch index doesn't include hashes in the same format as the wheels for torch. this results in tools like uv not storing hashes in the lockfile, which is a supply chain security concern. torch: [code block] triton: [code block] note that in the torch url the actual href ends with [inline code] , while that is missing from the triton urls. clearly in the triton case the sha is still computed since it's in these other metadata fields, but i guess that isn't part of the server spec (assuming uv is doing the right thing here). some of the torch urls don't have this, so maybe newer torch wheels have this issue too? more context from [pep503]( [url] the url should include a hash in the form of a url fragment with the following syntax: = , where is the lowercase name of the hash function (such as sha256) and is the hex encoded digest. versions current index cc

clean_text: torch.compile(dynamic=true) specializes batch dimension for layernorm backward, causing recompiles 🐛 describe the bug summary: when using [inline code] with [inline code] to trace a module containing [inline code] and [inline code] , and then compiling the resulting fx graph with [inline code] , the batch dimension ( [inline code] ) gets specialized. this causes a new recompilation for every new batch size, triggered by a "guard size mismatch at index 0". this behavior is specific to the presence of the [inline code] operator in the graph. a forward-only [inline code] graph compiles with a truly dynamic batch dimension as expected. background: i am working on a machine learning force field project and aim to accelerate my model's training process using torch.compile. briefly, the machine learning force field model reads distance vectors between atoms and outputs energy and forces, where the latter are derived by computing the gradient of energy with respect to the distance vectors. in other words, the forward() function of my model contains manually invoked autograd operations. additionally, the loss function of my model consists of two components (energy and forces), which means that higher-order gradients need to be computed during the backward pass of training. meanwhile, the "batch size" varies for each batch (since the number of atoms in each molecule is different), which requires that the code generated by compilation does not statically fix the dimensions of the input tensors. to address this, i first traced my computation graph using make fx(mode="symbolic"), then compiled the model with torch.compile(dynamic=true). however, i found that the compiled output still has statically fixed dimensions, leading to frequent recompilation when feeding inputs of different shapes. after analysis, i identified that the root cause of the recompilation is the backward operator of torch.nn.layernorm. a minimal reproducible example is provided below: minimal reproducer: [code block] [tl out.zip]( [url] error logs expected behavior: with [inline code] , the compiled model should handle a dynamic batch dimension ( [inline code] ) without recompiling. the first run (e.g., [inline code] ) should produce a compiled artifact where the batch dimension is treated as a [inline code] . subsequent runs with different [inline code] values (20, 30, etc.) should reuse this artifact without triggering any guard failures related to the batch dimension. actual behavior: for each new batch size [inline code] , a recompilation is triggered. the logs show a series of guard failures indicating that the batch dimension was specialized to the previous value. for example, when running with [inline code] after running with [inline code] through
