solution_comments: what’s broken is that torch.compile (torchdynamo + inductor) miscompiles dtype-changing view() operations by treating them as safe aliasing views, even though they reinterpret storage at the byte level. which is why compiling an unrelated bf16 path or adding a “no-op” reshape changes the q6 k result (the compiled kernel ends up reading misaligned or stale bytes, yielding 0.0625 instead of 1.0) the proper fix in pytorch is to treat dtype-changing views as unsafe, either force a materializing copy (or alignment/lifetime barrier) when view(dtype) changes element size, or tighten alias analysis so inductor cannot reuse storage across such reinterpret casts. a practical workaround is to avoid view(dtype) under torch.compile: replace it with to(dtype) (copy), or insert clone() / contiguous() right after any dtype reinterpretation, or keep gguf dequant code out of compiled regions; all of these prevent buffer reuse and restore correctness at the cost of a small copy. |SEP| does this diagnosis and proposed fix sound correct to you? should [inline code] that changes element size be treated as unsafe in inductor (copy-on-view or a no-alias/lifetime barrier), rather than a normal aliasing view, to prevent cross-graph buffer reuse and miscompilation in cases like gguf dequant? |SEP| i did root cause and added debug on above repro code. my observation is that some shapes in compile of qk 6 while we are using factory wrapper like (wrap compile) are created as dynamic shape. as compile think it is an extension of earlier compile. if i do add dynamo reset in between 2 compiles the code works as expected. i think this issue can be seen anywhere if someone is using factory wrapper but different closures. the fix is to add hashed closures as property in class codeid so that whenever we use different closures we generate different codeid. and , do let me know your opinion on the root cause and fix. |SEP| -in code review & audit: pr first off, excellent root-cause analysis. you successfully traced a very stealthy cache-poisoning bug back to its origin: dynamo treating dynamically generated functions (closures) as identical because it was only hashing the code object. the added test case (test factory created functions separate pgo state) is bulletproof and perfectly isolates the issue. however, while the fix works for this specific reproducer, there are a couple of system-level edge cases regarding python's cell semantics and distributed caching that we need to harden before this gets merged into main. here is my audit: 1. the valueerror blast radius (cell empty exception) in python, accessing c.cell contents on an uninitialized cell doesn't return none; it raises valueerror: cell is empty. in your implementation: [code block] because the try...except wraps the entire tuple comprehension, if even one cell in the closure is uninitialized, the exception is thrown, the comprehension aborts, and closure hash silently falls back to none. this leaves the function vulnerable to the exact same cache collision bug we are trying to fix if it happens to have mixed initialized/uninitialized cells. fix: handle the valueerror on a per-cell basis. 2. the ephemerality of id() and remote pgo in get closure content: [code block] using id(content) is a classic trap in caching layers. id() is tied to the memory address of the object, which is strictly tied to the lifecycle of the current python process. if a user is leveraging remote pgo (which relies on codeid to serialize state across distributed nodes or disk caches—see automatic dynamic remote pgo in pgo.py), id() will result in non-deterministic hashes across different runs. this will cause remote cache misses and trigger silent recompilations. fix: if the object isn't a code object, attempt to hash its actual value (if it is immutable/hashable) before falling back to id(). we want to preserve determinism as much as possible for remote caches. suggested code refactoring here is a hardened version of your logic that handles both the per-cell valueerror and improves the determinism of the hash: [code block] summary the pr is fundamentally correct in its approach. update the valueerror handling to prevent the whole hash generation from aborting. patch id() to prioritize actual object hashing for remote cache determinism. drop these updates in, and from an engineering perspective, this is a beautiful fix to a nasty bug. great job tracking this down!
